text: Four robotic ports are positioned approximately sixteen to eighteen centimeters below the xiphoid process, and an eight-centimeter retractor is used to elevate the liver for better access.

The robotic arms are equipped with a cardiac grasper and a bipolar vessel sealer, positioned to manipulate and seal vessels during the gastrectomy procedure.
Robotic arms one and two are equipped with a cardiac grasper and a bipolar vessel sealer, respectively, positioned for surgical manipulation.
The camera is positioned in arm three, slightly off the midline, while arm four is equipped with a fenestrated bipolar instrument.
The robotic camera is positioned in arm three, slightly off the midline, while arm four is equipped with a fenestrated bipolar instrument.
The laparoscopic assistant port is positioned between robotic arms three and four, facilitating access for the assistant during the gastrectomy procedure.

With the lesser sac widely open, the anesthesia team advances a lighted 40-french bougie with a gastro-protraction device, known commercially as a gastro-sail, into the stomach.

The surgeon detaches the posterior attachments of the stomach from the pancreas and retroperitoneum, typically without using energy devices.

The surgeon carefully navigates near the lesser curve to avoid opening the gastropathic ligament or injuring the left gastric vessels, ensuring precise dissection in the exposed lesser sac.
The surgeon navigates the close proximity of the spleen and stomach, carefully dissecting the tissue between these structures to avoid injury.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the angle of His, exposing the length of the left crus of the diaphragm to visually assess for any significant hernia.

The surgical team works to expose the entire length of the left crus of the diaphragm, ensuring the dissection is complete and visually confirming the absence of significant hernia.

The surgeon confirms the absence of esophageal temperature probes or gastric tubes, then begins stapling opposite the incisura to create a four-centimeter wide gastric channel.

The surgeon clamps the gastric tissue without using the bougie as a guide for the first staple fire, ensuring the anatomy is not distorted. After clamping, the bougie is advanced to confirm there is no obstruction.

The surgeon retracts the greater curvature of the stomach laterally, progressing towards the angle of His.

The surgeon maintains the stomach in a flat position to prevent corkscrewing of the staple line, carefully aligning the staple line near the fortifringe bougie without compressing the tissue around it.

The surgeon rotates the posterior aspect of the stomach while advancing along its length, ensuring proper orientation and exposure during the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs a leak test by injecting 100 cc of methylene blue mixed with saline through the aspiration channel on the bougie. Oxygen is then introduced under saline to visually confirm the absence of bubbles, indicating no leaks in the staple line.
